frozen_components: Language model (LLM used for inference only)
architecture.summary: The system proposes Globally Constrained Decoding (GCD) and Probabilistic GCD (P-GCD) for sampling from language models under logical constraints. GCD tensorizes finite automata (NFAs/DFAs) into matrices and uses forward-backward message passing to compute, at each decoding step, which tokens can lead to valid completions within a token budget, masking infeasible tokens. P-GCD augments this by distilling a hidden Markov model (HMM) approximating the LM and circuit-multiplying it with the tensorized FA using Kronecker operations, producing a joint HMM that encodes both hard constraints and probabilistic information. Both GCD and P-GCD are evaluated as proposal distributions and potentials within Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) sampling to approximate the constrained conditional p_lm(· | constraint).
trained_components: Hidden Markov Model (HMM) distilled to approximate LM distribution

biblio.title: Mitigating Bias in Locally Constrained Decoding via Tractable Proposals

claims.statement: Existing locally constrained decoding (LCD) approaches enforce constraints by myopically masking out next tokens, resulting in biased sampling and degradation in performance, and do not guarantee constraint satisfaction within a finite token budget.

Globally Constrained Decoding (GCD) guarantees that every generated length-T sequence satisfies the constraint by masking tokens that cannot lead to valid completions within the remaining token budget.
On xLAM function calling with JSON format, P-GCD achieves substantially higher accuracy than LCD at the same number of SMC particles, converging faster to the rejection-sampling ground truth.
GCD and P-GCD achieve 100.0% constraint satisfaction rate on xLAM (both JSON and Python-like formats) across particle budgets, while LCD drops to 95.26% to 99.20%.
LCD and GCD incur modest runtime overhead of 1.08× and 1.10× respectively compared to unconstrained generation on xLAM; P-GCD ranges from 3.11× to 10.2× slowdown depending on HMM hidden size.
On CommonGen keyword-based generation, P-GCD as a potential function achieves substantially higher BLEU-4 score than LCD proposals across a range of particle budgets.
On Spider text-to-SQL with 32 SMC particles, P-GCD achieves 67.9% execution accuracy compared to LCD at 65.4% and GCD at 66.8%.

For the SQL constraint grammar used in Spider experiments, the NFA has approximately 32k states at the token level, while the equivalent DFA construction exceeded available CPU memory, demonstrating the practical importance of NFA support.
